Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: Preterm infants are generally not recommended to receive RSV monoclonal antibodies during their second season. The risk for hospitalization doubles for extremely preterm infants in their second RSV season. Infants born extremely preterm could benefit from respiratory syncytial virus immunization during their second RSV season, according to findings published in JAMA Pediatrics. “The findings are not unexpected, but they contrast with prior recommendations in Canada and the United States, which until recently, did not support the use of RSV monoclonal antibodies for a second season in children born prematurely,” Pascal M. Lavoie, MDCM, PhD, a neonatologist at British Columbia Women’s Hospital, told Healio. Data derived from Paramo MV, et al. JAMA Pediatrics . 2026;doi:10.1001/jamapediatrics.2026.0035. If the maternal RSV vaccine (Abrysvo, Pfizer) was not given at least 14 days before delivery, the CDC and AAP recommend infants younger than 8 months receive nirsevimab (Beyfortus, AstraZeneca) or clesrovimab (Enflonsia, Merck) during their first RSV season. For children aged 8 through 19 months who are at high risk for severe RSV — including those who are immunocompromised, have chronic lung disease or are American Indian or Alaska Native — the CDC and AAP recommend nirsevimab for their second season as well. Recommendations are similar in Canada, although clesrovimab was approved by Health Canada earlier this year and is not yet included in national recommendations. Neither country recommends immunization for premature infants during their second season unless they have another medical condition that puts them at risk for severe disease. There has been little population-level research into RSV hospitalization for infants born prematurely, especially before 28 weeks’ gestation, and most studies do not extend past 12 months of age, Lavoie and colleagues wrote. The researchers conducted a birth cohort study of all children born in British Columbia, Canada, between April 1, 2013, and March 31, 2024, and collected RSV hospitalization data for up to 2 years after birth. Out of 443,221 infants (48.7% girls), 397,883 were born at 37 weeks’ gestational age or later, 44,209 were born between 28 and 36 weeks’ gestation, and 1,129 were born before 28 weeks’ gestation. Lavoie and colleagues noted that premature infants were much more likely to have chronic medical conditions than full-term infants. Overall, 4,649 infants (1%) were hospitalized for RSV before age 2 years, according to the authors. During their first RSV season, hospitalization rates were highest among infants born from 28 through 31 weeks’ gestation (22.55 per 1,000 person-years; 95% CI, 16.86-28.24) and lowest among full-term infants (6.95 per 1,000 person-years; 95% CI, 6.68-7.22). Hospitalization rates were lower during the second RSV season for most age groups, except for infants born before 28 weeks’ gestation, for whom hospitalization rates more than doubled (12.98 per 1,000 person-years; 95% CI, 6.25-19.71; vs. 28.97; 95% CI, 19.81-38.13). According to the researchers, RSV hospitalizations peaked before age 3 months among full-term infants, between 4 and 8 months for preterm infants and between age 15 and 17 months for extremely preterm infants. “In our view, the key message for pediatricians is that preterm infants continue to face a substantial risk of severe RSV disease requiring hospitalization beyond 12 to 15 months of age, particularly those born before 28 weeks’ gestation,” Lavoie said. “Accordingly, we advocate for the use of nirsevimab during their second RSV season.” For more information: Pascal M. Lavoie, MDCM, PhD, can be reached at plavoie@bcchr.ca . Sources/Disclosures Source: Paramo MV, et al. JAMA Pediatrics. 2026;doi:10.1001/jamapediatrics.2026.0035. References: AAP. Immunization guidance for infants and young children. https://www.cdc.gov/rsv/hcp/vaccine-clinical-guidance/infants-young-children.html . Updated Aug. 18, 2025. Accessed April 7, 2026. Health Canada. Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccines: Canadian immunization guide. https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/services/publications/healthy-living/canadian-immunization-guide-part-4-active-vaccines/respiratory-syncytial-virus.html . Updated April 2025. Accessed April 7, 2026. Merck. Key takeaways:
Preterm infants are generally not recommended to receive RSV monoclonal antibodies during their second season. The risk for hospitalization doubles for extremely preterm infants in their second RSV season.
Preterm infants are generally not recommended to receive RSV monoclonal antibodies during their second season.
The risk for hospitalization doubles for extremely preterm infants in their second RSV season.
Infants born extremely preterm could benefit from respiratory syncytial virus immunization during their second RSV season, according to findings published in JAMA Pediatrics.
respiratory syncytial virus immunization
JAMA Pediatrics.
“The findings are not unexpected, but they contrast with prior recommendations in Canada and the United States, which until recently, did not support the use of RSV monoclonal antibodies for a second season in children born prematurely,” Pascal M. Lavoie, MDCM, PhD, a neonatologist at British Columbia Women’s Hospital, told Healio.
Pascal M. Lavoie, MDCM, PhD,
Data derived from Paramo MV, et al. JAMA Pediatrics . 2026;doi:10.1001/jamapediatrics.2026.0035.
JAMA Pediatrics
If the maternal RSV vaccine (Abrysvo, Pfizer) was not given at least 14 days before delivery, the CDC and AAP recommend infants younger than 8 months receive nirsevimab (Beyfortus, AstraZeneca) or clesrovimab (Enflonsia, Merck) during their first RSV season. For children aged 8 through 19 months who are at high risk for severe RSV — including those who are immunocompromised, have chronic lung disease or are American Indian or Alaska Native — the CDC and AAP recommend nirsevimab for their second season as well.
nirsevimab
clesrovimab
Recommendations are similar in Canada, although clesrovimab was approved by Health Canada earlier this year and is not yet included in national recommendations. Neither country recommends immunization for premature infants during their second season unless they have another medical condition that puts them at risk for severe disease.
There has been little population-level research into RSV hospitalization for infants born prematurely, especially before 28 weeks’ gestation, and most studies do not extend past 12 months of age, Lavoie and colleagues wrote. The researchers conducted a birth cohort study of all children born in British Columbia, Canada, between April 1, 2013, and March 31, 2024, and collected RSV hospitalization data for up to 2 years after birth.
Out of 443,221 infants (48.7% girls), 397,883 were born at 37 weeks’ gestational age or later, 44,209 were born between 28 and 36 weeks’ gestation, and 1,129 were born before 28 weeks’ gestation. Lavoie and colleagues noted that premature infants were much more likely to have chronic medical conditions than full-term infants.
Overall, 4,649 infants (1%) were hospitalized for RSV before age 2 years, according to the authors. During their first RSV season, hospitalization rates were highest among infants born from 28 through 31 weeks’ gestation (22.55 per 1,000 person-years; 95% CI, 16.86-28.24) and lowest among full-term infants (6.95 per 1,000 person-years; 95% CI, 6.68-7.22). Hospitalization rates were lower during the second RSV season for most age groups, except for infants born before 28 weeks’ gestation, for whom hospitalization rates more than doubled (12.98 per 1,000 person-years; 95% CI, 6.25-19.71; vs. 28.97; 95% CI, 19.81-38.13).
According to the researchers, RSV hospitalizations peaked before age 3 months among full-term infants, between 4 and 8 months for preterm infants and between age 15 and 17 months for extremely preterm infants.
“In our view, the key message for pediatricians is that preterm infants continue to face a substantial risk of severe RSV disease requiring hospitalization beyond 12 to 15 months of age, particularly those born before 28 weeks’ gestation,” Lavoie said. “Accordingly, we advocate for the use of nirsevimab during their second RSV season.”

Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: Kyowa Kirin is discontinuing all clinical trials for the OX40 inhibitor rocatinlimab. The company reported one new confirmed case and one suspected case of Kaposi’s sarcoma. Perspective from Mona Shahriari, MD, FAAD Kyowa Kirin announced it is discontinuing all ongoing clinical trials for rocatinlimab, an investigational anti-OX40 monoclonal antibody, after a safety review identified “emerging concerns” of malignancies. Rocatinlimab, which targets memory T cells, was being assessed in multiple phase 2 and 3 trials for moderate to severe atopic dermatitis, moderate to severe uncontrolled asthma and prurigo nodularis. In a press release, the company said its most recent safety review conducted over the last several weeks identified “emerging concerns of malignancies with possible viral or immune-related links.” This included one new confirmed case and one suspected case of Kaposi’s sarcoma, in addition to the previously confirmed case, suggesting a potential mechanistic link to OX40 pathway modulation, according to the company. The company said that overall malignancy cases across the program remain below expected background rates; however, the characteristics of the cases “raise a plausible biological concern that cannot be excluded.” “This is deeply disappointing news, as we had hoped to bring a safe and effective treatment to patients,” Abdul Mullick, PhD, president and CEO of Kyowa Kirin, said in the release. “Rocatinlimab has demonstrated durable and clinically meaningful efficacy in moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis in the ROCKET program. However, the safety profile has evolved, and as patient safety remains our highest priority, we have taken this decisive and cautious step.” As Healio previously reported , data from the phase 3 trials, ROCKET-IGNITE and ROCKET-HORIZON, showed rocatinlimab improved moderate to severe dermatitis in adults, establishing the OX40 receptor as a validated treatment target. In those studies, reported treatment-emergent adverse events were similar between groups across both studies. The most common adverse events in the rocatinlimab arms were pyrexia, chills and aphthous ulcers, with pyrexia and chills attributed to injection-related reactions. “Although this outcome is not what we hoped for, our work has not been in vain,” Mullick said in the release. “The knowledge gained from the program will contribute meaningfully to the broader understanding of the OX40 pathway and future research efforts.” Kyowa Kirin discovered and advanced rocatinlimab, which was licensed to Amgen in 2021. In January, the companies announced they were terminating the commercialization partnership with Amgen, citing strategic portfolio prioritization. At that time, Kyowa Kirin said in a press release that a regulatory submission to the FDA for rocatinlimab was planned for the first half of 2026. Kyowa Kirin stated that both companies are notifying clinical trial investigators and regulatory authorities. After trial participants complete required safety follow-up visits, all studies will be formally terminated. The company stated that researchers will conduct a comprehensive analysis of the full dataset and will provide further updates after those assessments are complete. Perspective Back to Top Mona Shahriari, MD, FAAD Mona Shahriari, MD, FAAD OX40/OX40L targeting has been explored as an upstream strategy in atopic dermatitis with the aim of modulating sustained T-cell activation and immune memory. The discontinuation of rocatinlimab underscores the importance of rigorous, long-term safety evaluation — and reinforces the need to balance mechanistic promise with careful safety assessment — when developing therapies that alter costimulatory signaling. These findings add to our understanding of how best to approach immune recalibration in a complex, heterogeneous disease like AD. Mona Shahriari, MD, FAAD Associate Clinical Professor of Dermatology, Yale University School of Medicine Associate Director of Clinical Trials, CCD Research PLLC Founding Partner, Central Connecticut Dermatology Disclosures: Shahriari reports having financial relationships with AbbVie, Alumis, Amgen, Apogee, Arcutis, Bristol Myers Squibb, CorEvitas Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis Registry, Galderma, Incyte, Janssen, Leo Pharma, Lilly USA, Novartis, Organon, Oruka, Pfizer, Regeneron, Sanofi, Sanofi-Genzyme, Takeda and UCB. Sources/Disclosures Source: Press Release References: Guttman-Yassky E, et al. Lancet. 2025;doi:10.1016/s0140-6736(25)01865-3. Kyowa Kirin to regain control of rocatinlimab development commercialization program demonstrating strong commitment to address high unmet need in atopic dermatitis. Available at https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/01/30/3229497/0/en/Kyowa-Kirin-to-Regain-Control-of-Rocatinlimab-Development-and-Commercialization-Program-Demonstrating-Strong-Commitment-to-Address-High-Unmet-Medical-Need-in-Atopic-Dermatitis.html .
